zones.name: CLI End-to-End Tests
zones.description: Contains the full suite of end-to-end CLI tests covering adapter, analyze, import, prune, quiet mode, recommend, smart-add, sync, and other command paths.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero external coupling is the ideal profile for a test zone — these tests form a fully self-contained harness that does not pollute or depend on the production module graph.
With 13+ test files covering distinct CLI commands, confirm each file maps 1:1 to a CLI command module to maintain navigability as the command surface grows.
Absence of any entry points is correct for a test-only zone; ensure the vitest/jest config explicitly targets this directory so tests are never accidentally excluded from CI runs.
Cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0 confirm the e2e suite is architecturally isolated — an excellent baseline that should be preserved as new commands are added.

The breadth of CLI commands covered (adapter, analyze, import, prune, quiet, recommend, smart-add, sync plus 5 more) indicates strong end-to-end coverage; cross-reference with the CLI command list to identify any untested commands.
This zone is the only one with perfect cohesion in the entire codebase — a sign that the e2e layer has well-maintained boundaries compared to production zones.

The cli-e2e-tests zone (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0) exercises CLI commands that internally invoke notion-integration and integration-schemas logic, yet the e2e zone has zero direct coupling to either integration zone — this confirms the integration layer is correctly hidden behind CLI command abstractions, a clean layering win

cli-e2e-tests achieves coupling 0 despite covering adapter-related CLI commands (cli-adapter.test.ts, cli-sync.test.ts) — the integration layer is fully encapsulated behind CLI boundaries, which is the correct abstraction and should be preserved as the integration surface grows

cli-e2e-tests achieves coupling 0 by testing integration behavior exclusively through CLI stdout/exit-code assertions — this constrains test depth to observable CLI behavior only, meaning integration regressions that produce correct output but corrupt internal state (e.g. malformed prd.json after a sync) are structurally undetectable by this suite

cli-recommend.test.ts is the e2e test for the highest-risk command in the codebase (recommend.ts is the only command that bridges the analysis and persistence layers directly), yet the e2e suite can only assert on CLI stdout/exit-code — it cannot verify that acknowledged-findings.json was correctly written or that the PRD was not corrupted after a recommendation run. The most structurally complex command has the weakest test observability of any command in the suite.

Add a post-command file-state assertion to cli-recommend.test.ts: after running the recommend command, read acknowledged-findings.json and assert on its structure. This is the minimum additional coverage needed to catch persistence regressions in the highest-risk command without adding a full integration test layer.

report.ts is a CLI display command ([cli-command] archetype) whose natural peer group is status.ts, usage.ts, and health.ts in rex-status-mcp-cli, not the persistence/validation files in this zone — move report.ts to the display zone and rename data-persistence-validation to 'persistence-validation' to accurately reflect the remaining files' shared responsibility

zones.name: Integration Schemas
zones.description: Defines and exports the Zod/type schemas that describe the data shapes exchanged with external integrations (Jira, Notion) at the store boundary.
insights: High coupling (0.69) — 3 imports target "remote-integration"
Grouping Jira and Notion schemas under a shared index.ts barrel is a clean pattern — changes to either integration's schema have a single, obvious location.

Cohesion of 0.31 is below ideal; the Jira and Notion schemas are peers with no internal cross-dependency, so the community algorithm grouped them purely by shared consumers rather than internal coupling — this is acceptable for a schema barrel but worth noting.
Ensure the index.ts re-exports are kept minimal and explicit; schema barrels that grow unchecked tend to expose internal draft types and create hidden coupling for consumers.
Coupling 0.69 is high for a schema-only zone — schemas are being imported by many upstream modules, which is expected but means any schema shape change will have broad blast radius.

Cohesion 0.31 reflects two largely independent schema files grouped by external consumers rather than internal structure; this is architecturally acceptable for a schema barrel but indicates the zone boundary is consumer-driven, not domain-driven.

notion.ts lives here while the adapter and mapping logic live in the notion-integration zone — consider collocating or at minimum documenting that these two zones together form the full Notion integration layer.

integration-schemas is a pure-data zone (no logic, only shape declarations) while notion-integration is a behavioral zone (adapter + mapping logic) — this data/behavior split across zones is architecturally asymmetric and means the schema barrel is tightly coupled to the behavioral zone's consumers without being co-located with the code that interprets those schemas

integration-schemas (pure schema types) is consumed by remote-integration (3 imports) but has no import link to notion-integration despite both serving the same external service — schema and adapter are decoupled from each other while both couple to separate internal zones, a leaky abstraction at the service boundary

The index.ts barrel in integration-schemas re-exports both Jira and Notion schemas, but the Notion adapter lives in a separate zone with no import from this barrel — the barrel does not serve its natural consumer, undermining its purpose as a unified schema surface

integration-schemas does not appear as a source in cross-zone import traffic despite index.ts existing specifically to be consumed — this absence suggests the barrel's consumers are within the same algorithmic zone or consume it through file-level imports that bypass zone boundaries, making the barrel's actual downstream reach unverifiable from zone-level data alone

The index.ts barrel re-exports both jira.ts and notion.ts schemas but notion-integration (the natural consumer of notion.ts) has no import link to this barrel — the barrel's primary consumer for Notion schema types is some zone other than the adapter that interprets those schemas, inverting the expected schema→adapter data-flow

jira.ts has no counterpart behavioral zone — unlike Notion, which has notion-integration to house adapter and mapping logic, Jira has schema types only with no adapter zone anywhere in the codebase. If Jira adapter code is added, it will have no natural home and will likely accrete into whichever existing zone is most convenient at the time, repeating the fragmentation pattern of the Notion integration.

Pre-emptively create a jira-integration zone directory (src/store/jira/) before any Jira adapter code is written. Establishing the zone boundary before the code exists prevents the organic fragmentation that produced the notion-integration vs integration-schemas split. This is a process recommendation, not a code change.

zones.name: Mutation Commands

insights: Low cohesion (0.33) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.67) — 25 imports target "prd-operations-core"
This zone owns the write path of the CLI — every user-facing mutation command lives here, making it the right place to enforce input validation and consistent error messaging.
Coupling of 0.67 reflects that mutation commands depend on both core domain logic and the analyze pipeline, but contribute little back — this is architecturally correct for a command layer.

parent-completion.ts, parent-reset.ts, and timestamps.ts appearing as entry points suggests lifecycle side-effects are spread across multiple small modules; consolidating them into a single parent-lifecycle.ts would reduce surface area.
Cohesion of 0.33 and coupling of 0.67 flag this zone as a coordination layer rather than a cohesive domain — expected for a CLI command tier, but worth monitoring as commands are added.

12 imports from unit-cli into the broader cli zone and 10 reverse imports from cli into unit-cli indicate the boundary between these two CLI zones is not crisp — consider merging or more clearly separating concerns.
cli/errors.ts and cli/validate-input.ts as entry points are healthy signs — centralized error and validation handling prevents inconsistent user-facing messages across commands.
Zone "mutation-commands" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

mutation-commands depends on both core (domain primitives) and prd-analysis-pipeline (14 imports from unit-analyze into unit-cli) creating a diamond dependency: mutation commands reach through two layers to consume analysis internals, which should instead be mediated by a command-layer service or facade.

The 12 mutual imports between mutation-commands and the separate cli zone (10 cli→unit-cli, 12 unit-cli→cli) indicate the two CLI zones share internal helpers without a declared interface — each zone is importing from the other's internals rather than through a published API.

14 imports from unit-analyze into unit-cli (mutation-commands consuming analysis pipeline internals) create a layering violation: the command layer should call high-level service functions, not import from the analysis pipeline directly.

The 22 mutual imports between mutation-commands and the cli zone indicate they are effectively one zone with an arbitrary split boundary; the high bidirectional traffic should trigger a deliberate merge-or-separate decision rather than continued organic growth.

cli/help.ts is classified as [types] by the zone analyzer but contains runtime behavior: it imports formatHelp() from @n-dx/llm-client, builds COMMAND_DEFS records, and executes formatting logic. The misclassification causes the zone to under-report its runtime dependency on @n-dx/llm-client.

cli/index.ts (the CLI entry point) resides inside the mutation-commands zone — the entry point's zone placement means changing or testing the entry point requires reasoning about mutation-command-specific zone membership rather than a neutral orchestration boundary.

cli/help.ts is misclassified as [types] despite containing runtime formatting logic and an @n-dx/llm-client dependency. Archetype should be [utility] or [service] — the wrong classification distorts coupling metrics for this zone and may suppress findings about the cross-package dependency.

src/cli/index.ts (the CLI entry point) is zone-assigned to mutation-commands rather than to a dedicated entrypoint or orchestration zone. This couples the neutral entry point to the mutation-command zone boundary, making future extraction of mutation commands into a sub-package harder.

cli/errors.ts and cli/validate-input.ts are infrastructure utilities shared by all CLI commands, but their zone membership in mutation-commands means any CLI zone that imports them crosses a zone boundary. This creates accidental coupling from infrastructure-sharing: the more CLI commands grow, the more zone-boundary crossings accumulate from shared error and validation infrastructure rather than from genuine cross-domain logic.

src/recommend/ (3 files: types.ts, conflict-detection.ts, create-from-recommendations.ts) is a minimal three-file directory that earned its own zone designation. Its small size and the fact that recommend.ts CLI command imports from it directly suggests src/recommend/ is a satellite directory created to house analysis-adjacent logic that didn't fit analyze/ — a symptom of the same catch-all pattern as src/workflow/ and the misplaced acknowledge.ts.

cli/errors.ts and cli/validate-input.ts belong to all CLI commands equally but are zone-captured by mutation-commands. Extract them into a dedicated src/cli/infrastructure/ directory and assign them to a shared cli-infrastructure zone, making their cross-cutting role explicit and eliminating accidental zone-boundary crossings from infrastructure imports.

src/cli/commands/recommend.ts is classified as [cli-command] but is zone-assigned to remote-integration because it imports from acknowledge.ts — the zone algorithm followed import edges rather than the file's architectural role. A misclassified zone assignment for a CLI command module means coupling metrics for both remote-integration and mutation-commands are distorted: remote-integration appears to have CLI presence, and mutation-commands appears to lack it.

notion-map.ts lives in src/core/ but serves only notion-adapter.ts in src/store/ — a layer inversion where a utility is hosted above its sole consumer. Moving notion-map.ts to src/store/ (alongside its consumer) would correct both the physical misplacement and the test directory split, and would reduce the core zone's file count by one entry.

zones.name: Rex Runtime Data
zones.description: Persisted runtime state for the rex PRD tracker: project config, the PRD tree, execution log, and human-readable workflow state.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 4 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
These files are written by rex at runtime and read by both the CLI and MCP server — treat them as the source of truth for project state, not as source code.
prd.json and config.json should be kept in sync; divergence between them is the most common source of validation failures.
execution-log.jsonl grows unboundedly; consider a retention or rotation policy to prevent it from becoming a performance bottleneck.
Zero cohesion and coupling scores are expected for a data-only directory — the zone contains no TypeScript source files and no import edges.
workflow.md is the human-readable mirror of prd.json; any tooling that writes one should also update the other to keep them consistent.

All consumers parse prd.json and config.json at runtime with no compile-time type binding — a schema mismatch between writer and reader is invisible to TypeScript and will only surface as a runtime error or silent data corruption.

The zone's complete isolation from the import graph means static analysis tools cannot detect which modules depend on these files; a breaking change to prd.json structure has no automated blast-radius estimate.

Runtime data files are the only cross-layer coupling mechanism not visible in the import graph — every production zone that reads prd.json or config.json has an invisible dependency on this zone that bypasses all architectural boundary checks.

A Zod or JSON Schema validator applied at the single read-site (store load) would convert the invisible runtime coupling into an explicit, type-safe contract and catch schema drift at startup rather than mid-operation.

acknowledge.ts manages a second runtime data file (acknowledged-findings.json) outside this zone's tracked files — the rex-runtime-data zone is incomplete as a map of runtime state: it covers prd.json, config.json, execution-log.jsonl, and workflow.md but not acknowledged-findings.json, which is also written at runtime and read by the recommend command.

acknowledged-findings.json is a runtime-written state file not tracked in the rex-runtime-data zone. Any tooling or documentation that audits runtime state by inspecting this zone will miss a live data file, creating an invisible dependency gap identical to the one flagged for prd.json.

The .rex/ directory is the only directory in the package where all files are written at runtime with no compile-time guarantees, yet it has no designated owner zone capable of enforcing schema consistency across writes. Every production zone that writes to prd.json, execution-log.jsonl, or workflow.md does so as a side-effect with no shared write path — the runtime data directory is the most-written location in the package and simultaneously the least architecturally controlled.

The execution-log.jsonl schema is never validated on read — any zone can append an arbitrary JSON line and it will be accepted silently. Add a discriminated union type for execution log entries and validate on append rather than only on read, making the log schema a compile-time contract rather than a runtime convention.

The recommend CLI command (src/cli/commands/recommend.ts) imports from both the analysis pipeline (analysis logic) and acknowledge.ts (persistence) — it is the only command that bridges the analysis and persistence layers directly, making it the highest-risk site for a future regression if either layer changes its interface.

The absence of src/core/index.ts combined with mcp-tools.ts importing 18 core modules by path produces a structural anti-pattern: the MCP layer is the de-facto integration test for core module renaming — any core refactor must simultaneously update the MCP handler, making the transport layer the most fragile consumer of core internals rather than the most stable

store/index.ts serving dual roles (gateway barrel + factory logic) is a concrete violation of the project's own CLAUDE.md gateway rules — it is the only gateway in the codebase that breaks the re-export-only contract, and it does so in the highest-traffic store boundary

The three-layer chain (core modules → mcp-tools.ts → mcp.ts → MCP clients) has no stable interface between layers 1 and 2: mcp-tools.ts binds directly to 18 core paths. Adding a core operation query object or use-case interface between core and mcp-tools would eliminate the 18-import fan-in and align with the gateway pattern already used elsewhere in the monorepo

cli/index.ts dispatchAdd() implements mode detection using file-extension checking (extname) and flag inspection — this is domain routing logic in the orchestration entry point, a layer violation analogous to the constants.ts placement problem: the right fix is a command-mode resolver in src/cli/commands/add.ts or a dedicated src/cli/router.ts

Neither integration zone (notion-integration, integration-schemas) appears as a source in cross-zone import traffic — both are invisible leaves in the zone graph. This is architecturally correct for adapter code but means their public API surfaces cannot be validated through zone-level coupling metrics; a breaking export change in either zone will produce zero signal in cross-zone coupling reports.

The cross-zone import path cli→unit (20) combined with unit→core (32) forms a transitive three-hop chain: rex-status-mcp-cli→remote-integration→prd-operations-core. CLI rendering code has indirect access to all core domain logic through remote-integration as a structural pass-through, meaning a remote-integration refactor has a blast radius that extends into CLI presentation without any explicit import edge making this visible.

core (prd-operations-core) is the only zone that is simultaneously a major inbound hub (receives from unit-analyze: 49, cli: 35, unit-cli: 25, unit-store: 12, unit-core: 9) and a significant outbound exporter (to unit: 33, unit-analyze: 20, cli: 10) — this dual role makes it the single highest-risk zone for regression propagation: a breaking change to core's API surface fans out in all directions with no isolation layer in between.

unit-store (8 files, 12 imports into core, zero inbound from any zone) is the only production-adjacent zone in the cross-zone data with a provably unidirectional dependency profile — it should be used as the reference topology for store-layer zone design alongside unit-analyze (49 outbound, only 20 inbound).

The .rex (cohesion 0, 4 files) and root (cohesion 0, 9 files) zones have zero cohesion and appear in no cross-zone import traffic — they are configuration and entry-point file clusters with no structural role in the import graph. Their zone assignments are file-location artifacts rather than architectural groupings and their cohesion/coupling metrics should be excluded from package health aggregations.

The five algorithmically-named 'unit-*' zones (unit, unit-analyze, unit-cli, unit-core, unit-store) are structurally heterogeneous: 'unit' is a 30-file production zone (remote-integration), while unit-core and unit-store are test-only zones. Cohesion and coupling metrics across these five zones are not comparable without this distinction; averaging them obscures that the sole production 'unit-*' zone has the highest coupling in the group.

There is no integration test zone that validates notion-integration or integration-schemas programmatically — the only zone that exercises their behavior is cli-e2e-tests, which does so through CLI boundaries with zero direct coupling. Integration contract correctness for both Notion and Jira is entirely dependent on CLI-level output assertions, with no layer of programmatic integration tests between unit tests and e2e tests.

The rex package has no unified persistence abstraction: prd.json is managed through file-adapter.ts + atomic-write.ts, while acknowledged-findings.json is managed through raw fs/promises in acknowledge.ts. Adding a third runtime data file will require a third bespoke persistence implementation unless a shared store interface is introduced.

acknowledged-findings.json is a runtime state file produced by rex but absent from both the rex-runtime-data zone and the .rex/ directory (stored elsewhere by acknowledge.ts). The storage location for this file should be audited and standardized alongside prd.json and execution-log.jsonl to keep all runtime state co-located and zone-trackable.

The project's CLAUDE.md gateway rule ('Re-export only — gateways re-export; they contain no logic') is violated by store/index.ts, which is the highest-traffic store boundary in the package. This sets a precedent that other gateways (rex-gateway in hench, web gateways) may follow, eroding the architectural contract the rule is meant to enforce.

mcp-tools.ts creates a hidden 18-module dependency surface on core internals with no application layer. This is a missing abstraction: a use-case or operation-registry layer between the MCP transport (mcp.ts/mcp-tools.ts) and core domain logic would reduce the fan-in from 18 path-coupled imports to a single stable interface, consistent with the gateway pattern already enforced for cross-package imports.

core (prd-operations-core) acts as both the primary dependency hub and a significant consumer of other zones simultaneously — it receives from 5 zones and exports to 3. No other zone in the package has this dual hub-and-consumer profile. This makes core structurally non-isolatable: you cannot test it in isolation without mocking both its upstream providers and downstream consumers, and any interface change propagates in both directions

No production zone in the cross-zone import matrix has both zero inbound and zero outbound coupling simultaneously — every production zone participates in at least one bidirectional coupling relationship. This confirms there is no enforced one-way dependency contract anywhere in the production graph; the codebase has no architectural seam that is provably one-directional except at the leaf integration zones

notion-integration and integration-schemas have no programmatic consumers at the zone level and no dedicated integration test zone — their correctness is verified only through cli-e2e-tests (coupling 0, stdout-only). A schema shape change in integration-schemas or an adapter behavior change in notion-integration will not be caught by any test that asserts on internal state, only by tests that observe CLI output

The src/core/ directory is architecturally split between two zones (prd-operations-core and remote-integration), making it the only source directory in the package where the directory name is an unreliable predictor of zone membership. All other src/ subdirectories map cleanly to zone boundaries; core/ is the exception and the most likely source of future misplacements.

src/store/ conflates persistence infrastructure and integration adapters in a single directory under a name that only describes the first group. This naming gap means integration adapter files (notion-client.ts, notion-adapter.ts, adapter-registry.ts) will continue to accumulate in store/ as new integrations are added, progressively widening the semantic mismatch without triggering any static analysis warning.

The 'fix' zone (4 files, cohesion 0.25) has no src/fix/ directory — its members are scattered across other source directories and grouped by import patterns alone. A zone with no physical directory home is invisible to directory-based navigation and cannot be discovered without the zone map. This is the strongest indicator of an organic-accumulation zone that should be dissolved by redistributing its files to their appropriate directories.

src/core/notion-map.ts places Notion-domain mapping logic inside the core/ directory — a directory conventionally reserved for domain-agnostic primitives. If this file is zone-assigned to prd-operations-core, it creates a vendor-specific dependency inside the intended pure domain hub, meaning the core zone is no longer vendor-neutral and a Notion-specific change can propagate to every zone that imports from core.

Three minimal directories (src/recommend/ with 3 files, src/workflow/ with 1 file, and the directionless 'fix' zone) exist as namespace containers for modules that did not fit existing directories. These directories were created as a substitute for placing files in the right existing directory — each represents a decision to create structure rather than to use it, and each adds a zone designation that distorts cohesion metrics without adding architectural clarity.

The package has a persistent pattern of misclassified file archetypes (cli/help.ts as [types], cli/index.ts zone-assigned to mutation-commands, recommend.ts zone-assigned to remote-integration) — in every case the algorithm followed import edges rather than semantic role. The archetype classification system is not reliable enough to use as a primary input for zone health metrics without a manual audit pass on all [types]-classified files that have non-trivial runtime behavior.

No zone in the package corresponds to a dedicated 'infrastructure' layer for CLI concerns: error formatting, input validation, and help text generation are scattered across zone boundaries. This gap means every new CLI command must import infrastructure from a zone it doesn't logically belong to, creating perpetual zone-boundary crossings that are architectural noise rather than genuine coupling.

All three zones with BOTH low cohesion AND high coupling (rex-status-mcp-cli 0.33/0.67, prd-fix-command 0.25/0.75, task-selection-engine 0.17/0.83) share a single structural root cause: the absence of an application/use-case layer between the CLI+MCP surface and prd-operations-core. Introducing a thin operation-interface layer would simultaneously raise cohesion and lower coupling across all three zones without requiring them to be split individually.

The parallel src/core/X.ts + src/cli/commands/X.ts naming convention is applied inconsistently: some concepts are split (fix→fix, next-task→next, health→health) and others embed domain logic directly in CLI commands with no core counterpart. Without a documented criterion for when a core module is warranted, future contributors will apply the pattern arbitrarily, increasing the variance in abstraction depth across the CLI layer.

Display-layer files are split across two zones with no architectural rationale: status.ts, token-format.ts, status-sections.ts, health.ts, usage.ts live in rex-status-mcp-cli while report.ts lives in data-persistence-validation. A unified src/cli/display/ or src/cli/renderers/ layer would co-locate all rendering concerns, give them a shared home, and reduce the zone count by one through consolidation.

The package has no file whose sole responsibility is registering zone-to-directory mappings or enforcing directory structure — zone boundaries exist only in the community detection output. Without a src/ARCHITECTURE.md or an eslint boundary rule, each new file is placed by convention rather than enforcement, and zone drift is invisible until the next analysis run.

token-format.ts is consumed by three different commands (status-sections.ts, analyze.ts, usage.ts) making it a shared utility, yet it lives in a flat commands/ directory alongside command entry points — shared formatting utilities and command entry points are indistinguishable by location alone, making it unclear which files are entry points vs. helpers without reading each file's exports

core → unit-analyze (20 imports) and core → cli (10 imports) are upward dependencies from the domain foundation into consumer zones — the cross-zone matrix confirms that 'core' (prd-operations-core) is not a clean bottom-layer module but actively imports from zones above it in the intended hierarchy. These upward edges are the mechanical root cause of the bidirectional coupling findings already noted; the fix is not zone-level but file-level: identify which files in core are importing from unit-analyze or cli and relocate them to the appropriate consumer zone.

unit-analyze → unit-cli (14 imports) is a peer dependency between two test zones that serve different production zones (analysis pipeline vs mutation commands). Shared test fixtures or helpers should be extracted to a dedicated test-support zone (e.g., tests/support/) rather than creating cross-test-zone imports that make the analysis test suite dependent on the CLI mutation test suite.

The cross-zone import matrix contains no integration-test zone — all mid-level testing either happens in unit zones (isolated, mocked) or cli-e2e-tests (stdout-only). The absence of a programmatic integration test layer between unit and e2e is a structural gap that is especially acute for the two integration zones (notion-integration, integration-schemas) whose correctness is asserted only through CLI output.

The zone identifier 'unit' (assigned to remote-integration, a 30-file production zone) collides semantically with the 'unit-*' family (unit-analyze, unit-cli, unit-core, unit-store), which are test-adjacent zones. Cross-zone import reports that show 'unit → core' or 'core → unit' are ambiguous without knowing that 'unit' is the production remote-integration zone, not a test aggregation. Renaming the remote-integration zone's algorithmic ID to 'remote-integration' in tooling output would eliminate this ambiguity.

The bidirectional coupling between cli and unit-cli zones (10 + 12 crossings) mirrors the production core ↔ cli bidirectional pattern — the test zone structure inherited the layer-inversion problem from the production code it was written to test. Fixing the production layer inversion (core → cli) would create the opportunity to simultaneously fix the test zone inversion (cli ↔ unit-cli) in the same refactor pass.

The src/core/ directory is zone-split (canonical.ts/dag.ts/move.ts/tree.ts in remote-integration; remainder in prd-operations-core). Add a lint rule or CI check that flags any new file added to src/core/ without a corresponding zone-map update, preventing silent misclassification of future core modules.

src/core/notion-map.ts introduces a vendor-specific dependency into the directory conventionally reserved for domain-agnostic primitives. Relocate notion-map.ts to src/store/ alongside notion-adapter.ts to keep src/core/ vendor-neutral, or document explicitly that core/ permits vendor-specific helpers — leaving the current state undocumented perpetuates the pattern.

The 'fix' zone (4 files) has no corresponding src/fix/ directory, making it undiscoverable by directory navigation. Identify the 4 member files, redistribute them to their semantically correct directories (core/, cli/commands/, etc.), and dissolve the zone. A zone with no physical directory home is an architectural liability, not an asset.

src/recommend/ (3 files) and src/workflow/ (1 file) are minimal directories with no zone-level justification. Absorb src/recommend/ files into src/analyze/ (conflict-detection, create-from-recommendations) and src/schema/ (types.ts), and move src/workflow/default.ts into src/core/ or src/store/ based on its import profile. Eliminating trivial directories reduces zone fragmentation and improves metric reliability.

The archetype classification [types] is applied to files with runtime behavior (cli/help.ts imports formatHelp() from @n-dx/llm-client; other examples likely exist). Run a targeted audit of all [types]-classified files and reclassify any that contain non-trivial imports or executable logic — the misclassification systematically understates coupling scores for zones that contain these files.

Introduce an explicit archetype-to-directory mapping for src/cli/: entry-point commands in src/cli/commands/, shared display helpers in src/cli/renderers/ (token-format.ts, status-sections.ts), and MCP transport files in src/cli/mcp/ (mcp.ts, mcp-tools.ts). This three-subdirectory split maps directly onto the three concerns currently blended in rex-status-mcp-cli and would raise cohesion by making file placement self-documenting.

The 'core module required' decision has no documented criterion — currently applied to fix (1 consumer), next-task (2+ consumers), health (3 consumers) without a visible rule. Document in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md: 'A src/core/X.ts module is warranted when (a) two or more consumers exist, or (b) the logic is independently unit-tested with 3+ test cases.' This makes single-consumer core modules (fix.ts) visible as candidates for consolidation.

Five files named identically across two directory levels exist in the package (fix.ts×2, health.ts×2, next/next-task asymmetry, stats.ts×?) — adopt a naming convention where core domain modules use a noun describing the domain concept (tree, stats, health-score) and CLI command wrappers use an imperative verb (fix-cmd.ts, report.ts, next.ts). This eliminates ambiguity in stack traces and grep output without changing public APIs.

The cross-zone import matrix shows core exporting to both unit-analyze (20) and cli (10) — these are upward imports from the domain hub into consumer zones. Audit src/core/ for any file that imports from src/cli/ or src/analyze/ and relocate those files to the appropriate consumer zone. This is the single concrete action that would eliminate the most bidirectional coupling findings without any zone boundary changes.

Introduce a tests/support/ directory for shared test fixtures and utilities. The unit-analyze → unit-cli (14) cross-test-zone dependency indicates that test helpers are currently hosted in a test zone they do not conceptually belong to. A shared support zone with zero production coupling would absorb these helpers and reduce cross-test-zone coupling to near zero.

The algorithmic zone ID 'unit' refers to the 30-file production remote-integration zone, creating a naming collision with the unit-* test zones throughout all tooling output. Assign a stable alias 'remote-integration' to this zone in the zone configuration to prevent cross-zone coupling reports from being misread as test-layer data.

No production zone achieves both high cohesion (>0.5) and low coupling (<0.3) simultaneously. The minimum intervention to create the first such zone is to isolate the task-selection-engine zone: it already has 5 dedicated test files and a unidirectional import profile (9 outbound to core, 1 inbound). Introducing a gateway or index.ts barrel for task-selection-engine would formalize this boundary and produce the package's first provably low-coupling production zone.
